thinking.text:
I have 169 raw commands. The game had 262 moves, so I'm missing about 93 commands. The issue is that the 1fps frame rate only captures 190 samples over 190 seconds, but the player types 262 commands, meaning they type faster than 1 command per second on average. I need to process more frames (5fps) to capture all commands.

However, processing all 951 HQ frames with OCR would take too long. Let me think of an alternative approach.

Actually, looking at the transcript structure more carefully, each frame shows MULTIPLE previously executed commands in the transcript. The transcript scrolls, showing maybe 5-10 commands at a time. I should extract ALL commands from each frame's transcript (not just the typing line) and track the sequence by detecting when new commands appear in the transcript compared to the previous frame.

Let me re-examine: frame_0013 shows transcript commands: n, n, u and typing: get
frame_0014 shows transcript commands: d, s, e, . and typing: .

So between frames 13 and 14, the commands 'get' was executed (it was typing in 13), and then 'd', 's', 'e', '.' were typed (but only '.' is shown as current typing in 14). Wait, that doesn't work either.

Looking at the actual game: the transcript shows ALL commands that have been executed so far. In frame 13, the executed commands shown are: n, n, u. In frame 14, the executed commands shown are: d, s, e, . . This means the transcript in frame 14 no longer shows n, n, u - it scrolled off. So each frame only shows a WINDOW into the full transcript.

I need to track the FULL sequence by looking at each frame and seeing which commands are visible. The commands scroll from bottom to top as new ones are added.

Actually, looking at the game more carefully: each command-line prompt '>' shows a command followed by the game's response. The game response is multi-line. The '>' lines are the commands themselves. So the transcript is a sequence of:
>command
game response...
>next command
game response...
...

So if I extract ALL '>' lines from each frame, I get the commands that are currently visible in the transcript window. By tracking changes between frames, I can identify when new commands scroll into view.

But the problem is that each frame only shows a few commands (maybe 5-10 lines of '>' commands). So I need to combine information from multiple frames to reconstruct the full sequence.

Let me try a different approach: for each frame, extract the LAST '>' line (which is the command being typed) and the SECOND-TO-LAST '>' line (which is the most recently completed command). Track when the most recently completed command changes - that gives us the sequence of executed commands.

Let me examine the data more carefully.
